prompt: organizer.py is the little rules-driven tidier a few of us point at our downloads folders (rules map extensions to a dest folder, dest can be a strftime pattern off mtime, shallow sweep, first rule wins). It works, but people are scared to run it, and honestly they're right to be: if images/photo.jpg already exists it just clobbers it, there's no way to preview what a run would do, and no way back after a fat-fingered rule. Ticket has three parts. (1) Collision policies: organize() takes on_collision with 'skip' (leave the source where it is), 'rename' (append _1, _2, ... before the extension, first number that's free — and 'free' has to account for targets already promised earlier in the same run, not just what's on disk), or 'overwrite'. Default 'rename'. (2) Dry run: a new plan(root, rules, on_collision=...) returns the action list as (action, src, dst) tuples — action is 'move', 'skip' or 'overwrite', renames show up as 'move' with the suffixed dst — and organize(..., dry_run=True) returns that same plan while touching absolutely nothing. (3) Undo: every real run writes .organize-journal.json at the root recording the executed moves as {src, dst} objects (skips don't count, and each run replaces the journal — last run only), and undo_last(root) walks it in reverse moving files back, silently skipping anything that's since disappeared, returns the (dst, src) pairs it restored, and deletes the journal; no journal at all is a ValueError. CLI grows --on-collision, --dry-run (prints 'ACTION src -> dst' lines) and --undo. Existing behavior — return format of organize(), scan order, dotfiles/subdirs left alone, date buckets — must not change. test_organizer.py (python3 test_organizer.py) is the acceptance contract; don't edit it.
